intake.finding: ChromeStatus entry created 2026-05-10 by cgqaq@chromium.org with a concrete, spec-grounded problem statement: CSS lacks a primitive transparent/solid-color <image>, forcing workarounds like linear-gradient(transparent); the gap blocks correct light-dark(..., none) round-tripping and registered <image> custom properties. Owner, component (Blink>CSS), spec link, tracking bug, and motivation are all present and internally consistent. Feature is classified 'Chromium catches up' to CSS Images L4.
intake.limitations: 'Initial public proposal' is 'No information provided' and there is no Chrome-authored explainer; the problem is asserted via CSS WG resolution rather than Chrome-led developer research.

incubation.finding: Incubation occurred in the CSS Working Group, not as a Chrome-specific design: the spec (CSS Images L4) already defined image(), the WG resolved (w3c/csswg-drafts#12513) that light-dark(..., none) computes to image(transparent), and image() was narrowed to image(<color>) with the broader syntax moved to css-images-5. The use case (transparent-image primitive, light-dark round-trip, custom-property initial values, overlay idiom) is real and WG-validated.
incubation.limitations: No Chrome explainer, no TAG review (marked 'Not applicable'), and 'Web developers: No signals' in the record, so Chrome did not independently validate demand; it relied on WG/spec consensus and the light-dark() dependency.

prototype.finding: An Intent to Prototype was filed (ChromeStatus stage 220, referenced from the Intent to Ship) and an implementation exists behind the CSSImageFunction runtime feature with WPT parsing tests (image-function-valid/computed/invalid). The grammar is minimal (image() = image(<color>)), reducing prototype risk.
prototype.limitations: No public friction log or framework/server integration spike was found; prototype validation appears to rest on the spec and WPT rather than task-based testing. The Intent to Prototype thread itself could not be read directly (reader rejected its URL-encoded msgid), so its content is inferred from the Intent to Ship reference.

developerTrials.finding: No developer trial was conducted: the manifest contains only the milestone-150 'Enabled by default' event, with no behind-flag dev-trial stage. For a narrow, spec-stable CSS parsing/rendering primitive with WPT coverage and pre-existing cross-engine implementation, a recruited developer-trial cohort was not warranted.
developerTrials.limitations: Consequently no external pre-ship developer feedback, ergonomic findings, or integration evidence was gathered through a trial; the only independent developer signal found post-ship is community coverage (S9).

wideReview.finding: Wide review was rigorous on the Chromium side and surfaced a real interop defect. TAG review was marked 'Not applicable'. The blink-dev Intent to Ship disclosed Gecko as 'Shipped/Shipping' and WebKit as 'No signal', but Dan Clark (Microsoft/Edge) noted image()/light-dark(none) WPTs still failed on Firefox Stable 151; Emilio Cobos Álvarez (Mozilla) clarified that Firefox's light-dark(none) ship was an internal-representation accident and that image(<color>) proper only lands in Firefox 152. LGTMs then followed from Dan Clark, Mike Taylor, and Yoav Weiss. WebKit has no written position: standards-positions #658 (light-dark with images) shows 'WebKittens: No response' and #402 (image() itself, opened 2024-09-23) also has no WebKit reply.
wideReview.limitations: No formal WebKit/Safari position exists for image(); the WebKit signals tracked in ChromeStatus actually point at the adjacent light-dark-with-images issue (#658), not image() directly. The Chromium 'position identified' project label on #658 is not a published WebKit stance.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial or field experiment was run (experiment_timeline null; no OT stage; no Intent to Experiment). There were no field-only uncertainties to resolve for a self-contained parsing/rendering primitive whose behavior is fully specified and WPT-covered.
experiment.limitations: No field compatibility or real-origin telemetry was collected; compatibility risk was assessed via WPT and cross-engine review rather than live exposure.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship (2026-05-26) reached 3+ LGTMs by 2026-05-27 after the interop clarification. Plan: ship enabled for all users via Finch 'CSSImageFunction' on desktop/Android/WebView in milestone 150, all six Blink platforms, no //chrome code, no enterprise impact, no breaking change. The Chrome 150 release notes list the feature with an accurate description and links to tracking bug, ChromeStatus, and spec.
prepareToShip.limitations: Cached ChromeStatus (accurate_as_of 2026-05-10) still shows security_review_status and privacy_review_status as 'Pending'; their final states at ship could not be confirmed from public sources. Chromium tracking bug 510426954 contents were not readable via the reader.

release.finding: Chrome 150 stable released 2026-06-30 with the feature included; the release notes describe image(<color>) accurately ('generate a solid-color image from any color; image() = image(<color>)') alongside the separate but related 'CSS light-dark() with image values' feature. Claims match the actual rollout (desktop/Android/WebView). A Chrome 150 beta announcement was made. The related Firefox 152 release (2026-06-16) shipped image(<color>) per Mozilla, so Chrome shipped into a recently-interoperable state.
release.limitations: No dedicated Chrome blog/article or sample was published for image() specifically; it is a minor release-notes item. MDN reference docs were stale at release (see evidenceAndCommunication).

adoption.finding: Too early and no data to score. The feature shipped ~3 weeks before the evidence cutoff. ChromeStatus records 'Web developers: No signals' and no use-counter/telemetry, framework, library, or tool-integration evidence was found. Independent grassroots interest exists: a June 2026 community post demonstrates a concrete overlay use case (stacking a semi-transparent color over a photo in background-image layers) and notes the color behaves as a real image that keeps text selectable.
adoption.limitations: Cannot infer adoption from shipment or from a single community post. No Chrome use-counter bucket, HTTP Archive/web-features Baseline status, or framework/library support data was available within the cutoff.

support.finding: No Chrome-specific support burden, known issues, or regressions were found within the cutoff. The only friction signal is interop/WPT, not support load: a Mozilla bug commenter notes Firefox fails one css-color light-dark-image WPT and disputes the expected result, filing an issue.
support.limitations: Feature is days-to-weeks old; support ticket volume, recurrence, and resolution time are unknown. The WPT disagreement concerns the dependent light-dark() behavior, not image(<color>) parsing directly.

deprecation.finding: This is a new feature addition, not a deprecation or removal: no existing API is restricted, removed, or put behind a reverse trial. The Chrome 150 release notes explicitly state 'There are no deprecations and removals in this release.'
deprecation.limitations: Not applicable; included only to replay the phase explicitly.

developerValue.finding: The value is concrete and demonstrated. image(<color>) gives an unambiguous <image> for transparent/solid-color cases, fixes the light-dark(..., none) round-trip, supplies a valid initial value for registered <image> custom properties, and replaces the linear-gradient(transparent)/linear-gradient(red,red) idiom with a self-documenting expression. An independent working demo (overlay a semi-transparent color over a photo, keeping text selectable) confirms genuine ergonomic value, and the motivation was CSS-WG-validated.
developerValue.limitations: Narrow scope: it is a 'Chromium catches up' ergonomic/correctness primitive, not a capability that was previously impossible (the overlay/transparent cases had workarounds). Formal 'Web developers: No signals' means it was not demand-validated through Chrome research.

endUserImpact.finding: Direct end-user impact is negligible by nature (a CSS authoring primitive). Indirect benefit is more correct dark-mode/light-dark() behavior when none is the chosen branch. No accessibility, privacy, or security regression is evidenced; background-image semantics around assistive technology are unchanged.
endUserImpact.limitations: No measurable user-facing outcome; scored unscored rather than inferring success from absence of harm.

adoption.finding: Cannot be scored: feature shipped 2026-06-30, ~3 weeks before cutoff; no usage telemetry, use-counter bucket, framework/library integration, or Baseline status was available. One independent community demo exists but cannot represent adoption.
adoption.limitations: Per method, shipment and a single community post are not adoption. Revisit after telemetry and HTTP Archive/web-features data mature.

interoperability.finding: Two engines now ship image(<color>): Chrome 150 (2026-06-30) and Firefox 152 (2026-06-16, per Mozilla's confirmation on blink-dev). Safari has it in Safari Technology Preview 246 (community-verified) but there is no confirmation it is in Safari stable and no formal WebKit standards position. The spec (CSS Images L4) is a Working Draft / Editor's Draft. WPT parsing tests exist. One WPT disagreement on the dependent light-dark-image test was raised against Firefox.
interoperability.limitations: The raw cross-browser WPT pass/fail matrix could not be retrieved (wpt.fyi is JS-rendered and its API errored); interop status rests on qualitative evidence. Safari stable status and Baseline 'widely available' timing remain open.

implementationQuality.finding: Implementation is backed by WPT parsing tests (image-function-valid/computed/invalid), shipped cleanly on all six Blink platforms with no crashes or regressions reported, and withstood cross-engine LGTM scrutiny (including a Firefox-stable WPT check). The minimal grammar limits implementation risk.
implementationQuality.limitations: Current cross-browser WPT pass/fail results were not obtainable; no public performance, edge-case, or computed-value fidelity data was reviewed beyond the existence of the tests.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: The blink-dev Intent to Ship was high-quality and transparent: motivation well-argued, browser positions disclosed, and the Firefox interop discrepancy openly surfaced and resolved before LGTM. Chrome 150 release notes are accurate. However, the ChromeStatus standards-position fields were imprecisely attributed: both the Gecko 'Shipped/Shipping' (bugzilla 2023569) and WebKit 'No signal' (standards-positions #658) point at the adjacent light-dark-with-images feature, not image() itself, which reviewers had to manually de-risk. MDN reference docs were stale at launch, still showing the old multi-argument image() grammar.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: The conflation of adjacent-feature positions is a real communication-precision gap; MDN staleness is community-doc lag, not a Chrome claim, but it affects developer accuracy at launch.

support.finding: Insufficient elapsed time and no data: no Chrome support issues, recurrence, or resolution metrics found within cutoff. The only signal is a disputed light-dark-image WPT against Firefox (interop, not support burden).
support.limitations: Too early to assess support burden; revisit after stable has been in the field.

overall.finding: A clean, transparent, on-schedule launch of a narrow, spec-grounded CSS catch-up primitive, with rigorous cross-engine review that caught and corrected an interop signal error, and with two engines shipping within two weeks. But mixed rather than successful overall: Safari has no formal position and only preview implementation (stable unconfirmed), the spec is still a Working Draft, ChromeStatus standards-position signals were attributed to the adjacent light-dark feature, MDN was stale at launch, and adoption/end-user outcomes are unscored due to recency and absent telemetry.
overall.limitations: Overall does not hide the unscored adoption/support dimensions or the incomplete interop picture; success of the launch process is distinct from durable outcome success.

supports: Feature scope image(<color>), Blink>CSS, Chromium catches up, CSSImageFunction finch, ship desktop/android/webview 150
Standards positions Gecko Shipped/Shipping (bug 2023569) and WebKit No signal (#658), both annotated 'light-dark(none,none) depends on image(none)'
Motivation: transparent-image primitive, light-dark() round-trip, registered <image> custom properties, replaces linear-gradient(transparent)
WPT links, tag_review_status Not applicable, security/privacy review Pending (pre-ship snapshot)
sources.limitations: Snapshot accurate_as_of 2026-05-10 (pre-ship); is_released=false in the cached copy. Browser-signal URLs point at the dependent light-dark feature rather than image() directly.

sources.publisher: W3C CSS Working Group (CSS Images Module Level 4, Editor's Draft 7 May 2026)
supports: image() = image(<color>); represents a solid-color image of the specified color with no natural dimensions
Note: prior additional image() behaviors shifted to css-images-5 (scope narrowed)
<image> type grammar; Working Draft / Editor's Draft maturity
Editors: Tab Atkins Jr. (Google), Elika Etemad/fantasai (Apple), Lea Verou
sources.limitations: Editor's Draft, explicitly non-stable ('may change at any moment').

supports: 'Ship light-dark() for images' resolved/fixed in Firefox 150 (emilio), resolved via w3c/csswg-drafts#12513
Firefox release metadata indicates Firefox 152.0 released 2026-06-16 (LATEST_FIREFOX_VERSION 152.0.6, LAST_RELEASE_DATE 2026-06-16)
Commenter notes Firefox fails one css-color light-dark-image WPT and disputes the expected result
Release note wording: light-dark() on images for dark mode
sources.limitations: This bug is about light-dark() for images (the dependent feature in Firefox 150), not image(<color>) directly; image() proper landed later (Firefox 152 per S6). '4 months ago' relative timestamps are approximate.

supports: 'CSS light-dark() with image values' opened 2026-04-29 by CGQAQ; WebKittens: No response in the issue body
Notes Mozilla already ships it (bug 2023569) and that it matches Firefox's implementation
Project field shows 'Position identified' but no published WebKit stance appears in the issue
sources.limitations: This is the light-dark-with-images position request that ChromeStatus cites as the WebKit signal; it is not a position on image(<color>) itself, and contains no written WebKit position.

supports: Earlier, direct request for a WebKit position on CSS Images L4 image() (opened 2024-09-23 by jsnkuhn)
WebKittens: No response; references mozilla/standards-positions#856 and WebKit bug 72584
Describes the broader old image() (spriting, solid color via image(red), fallbacks) rather than the narrowed image(<color>)
sources.limitations: No WebKit position identified; long-standing open request, not acted on publicly.

supports: Intent to Ship CSS image(<color>) 2026-05-26 by cgqaq; ship desktop/android/webview 150; Finch CSSImageFunction; all six Blink platforms
Disclosed Gecko Shipped/Shipping (bug 2023569) and WebKit No signal (#658) with 'light-dark(none,none) depends on image(none)' notes
Dan Clark (Microsoft/Edge): image()/light-dark(none) WPTs fail on Firefox Stable 151; requested Mozilla confirmation
Emilio Cobos Álvarez (Mozilla): Firefox's light-dark(none) ship was an internal-representation accident; image(<color>) implemented after CSSWG discussion and should ship in Firefox 152
LGTMs from Dan Clark, Mike Taylor, Yoav Weiss (2026-05-27); references Intent to Prototype thread
sources.limitations: Reader rejected the URL-encoded msgid form; accessed via the @ form. The referenced Intent to Prototype thread could not be read directly.

supports: Chrome 150 stable release date 2026-06-30; applies to Android, ChromeOS, Linux, macOS, Windows
'CSS image(<color>) function' listed with accurate description and tracking bug 510426954 + ChromeStatus + spec links
Separate 'CSS light-dark() with image values' also shipped in 150 (tracking bug 491829958)
'There are no deprecations and removals in this release'
sources.limitations: Release notes; descriptive, not a measurement of adoption or quality.

supports: MDN <image> page last modified 2025-10-10 (before Chrome 150 ship)
Formal syntax still shows the OLD multi-argument image(): image(<image-tags>? [<image-src>?, <color>?]!), not the narrowed image(<color>)
Notes background images are not announced to assistive technology (accessibility consideration)
sources.limitations: Community-maintained; staleness is a doc-lag observation, not a Chrome claim. Browser-compat table did not render (JS) and was not captured.

supports: image() supported in Safari Technology Preview 246
Concrete use case: stack a semi-transparent color over a photo using background-image layers (image(rgb(0 0 0 / 25%)), url(photo.png))
Comment (Schalk Neethling): overlay only affects the image, text remains selectable, avoiding pseudo-element/z-index workarounds
Demo by Tim Nguyen referenced
sources.limitations: Single grassroots post; not representative of broad adoption. Confirms STP 246 support but not Safari stable status.

supports: Chrome 150 beta announced with CSS styling/navigation features
sources.limitations: Exact post date not captured; only search snippet available. Corroborates beta timing, not the feature itself.

successes: Transparent, high-quality Intent to Ship: motivation well-argued, positions disclosed, and a real interop signal error (Firefox 'Shipped' was actually the light-dark(none) accident; image(<color>) proper only in Firefox 152) was caught by cross-engine review (Dan Clark) and resolved by Mozilla (Emilio) before LGTMs (S6).

Clean, on-schedule ship on all six Blink platforms (Chrome 150 stable 2026-06-30) with accurate release notes, correct scope (image(<color>) only, broader image() deferred to css-images-5), and WPT coverage (S7, S2, S1).
Cross-engine alignment achieved quickly: Firefox 152 shipped image(<color>) on 2026-06-16, ~2 weeks before Chrome 150; Safari Technology Preview 246 also supports it (S6, S3, S9).
Independent validation of genuine developer value via a working overlay use case that keeps text selectable (S9).
failures: ChromeStatus standards-position signals were attributed to the adjacent light-dark-with-images feature, not image() itself: both the Gecko 'Shipped/Shipping' (bug 2023569) and WebKit 'No signal' (#658) point at light-dark issues, producing an imprecise interop picture that reviewers had to manually correct (S1, S4, S6).
No formal WebKit/Safari position on image() (both #402 and #658 unanswered), and Safari stable status for image(<color>) is unconfirmed (only Technology Preview 246 verified) (S4, S5, S9).
MDN reference documentation was stale at launch, still describing the old multi-argument image() grammar rather than the narrowed image(<color>) (S8).
No usage/adoption telemetry, framework/library/tool integration, or 'web developer signals' were available; the feature shipped with 'Web developers: No signals' (S1).
counterfactuals: If Dan Clark had not checked the image()/light-dark(none) WPTs against Firefox Stable 151, Chrome could have shipped believing Firefox already supported image(<color>), and launch communications could have overstated interoperability; the manual WPT check was the safeguard (S6).
If the WebKit standards-position request had targeted image() directly (rather than the conflated light-dark-with-images #658), the interop picture and Safari intent would be clearer today (S4, S5).

If a Chrome-authored explainer or sample had existed (explainer_links is empty), the overlay/clean-up use cases might have surfaced earlier and generated the developer signals that were absent, and MDN might have been updated sooner (S1, S8, S9).
A dedicated Chrome use-counter bucket for image() (webdx_usecounter_enum is null) would let adoption be measured post-ship; without it, adoption remains unscored (S1).
skillImprovements: Phase 04 (wide review) rule: when a feature's primary motivation depends on another feature (here light-dark()), the standards-position fields must cite a position for THIS feature, not the dependency; the transition packet should flag adjacent-feature conflation explicitly.

Phase 06 (prepare-to-ship) check: reconcile each ChromeStatus browser-signal URL against the actual feature scope before claiming 'Shipped/Shipping'; here the Gecko signal pointed at a light-dark bug and required manual WPT de-risking.
Phase 07 (release) check: verify MDN/caniuse reflect the narrowed spec at launch and file a docs PR for stale formal syntax (old multi-argument image()) before stable.

Add a retrospective eval case for 'spec catch-up CSS primitive' so narrow ergonomic features are neither over-scored as broad successes nor allowed to inherit adjacent-feature positions as their own signals.
Encourage registering a use-counter bucket at Intent to Ship for features whose adoption is otherwise unmeasurable, so post-launch outcome assessment is not blocked.

missingEvidence: Raw cross-browser WPT pass/fail matrix for image-function-valid/computed/invalid (wpt.fyi is JS-rendered and its results API returned a 500; only qualitative WPT evidence from the intent thread was obtained).
Confirmed Safari STABLE version shipping image(<color>) (only Safari Technology Preview 246 was verified via community source; no formal WebKit standards position and no stable confirmation).
Intent to Prototype thread content (reader rejected its URL-encoded msgid; inferred only from the Intent to Ship reference).

Any Chrome use-counter/usage telemetry, HTTP Archive/web-features Baseline status, or framework/library/tool integration evidence (feature ~3 weeks old at cutoff; none found; use-counter enum is null).
Chromium tracking bug 510426954 contents and final security/privacy review states at ship (cached record showed both as 'Pending').
